docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Cloud Native GIS is a platform for handling layers and served on map.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
Layer can be in vector and raster and being served as tile.

Style is saved in database. Vector layer is in mapbox style.

Maputnik is being used to change the style.

docs.title: Production
docs.text:
git submodule update
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/CloudNativeGIS
cp deployment/.template.env deployment/.env
cp deployment/docker-compose.override.template deployment/docker-compose.template
make up

The web will be available at http://127.0.0.1/

To stop containers:

make kill

To stop and delete containers:

make down

docs.title: Install as django library
docs.text:
This application can be installed as django library.

To install it, put git+https://github.com/kartoza/CloudNativeGIS.git to requirements. Add cloud_native_gis to INSTALLED_APPS, and add url path('', include('cloud_native_gis.urls')).

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
git submodule update
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/CloudNativeGIS
cp deployment/.template.env deployment/.env
cp deployment/docker-compose.override.template deployment/docker-compose.template

After that, do

  • open new terminal
  • on folder root of project, do
make serve

Wait until it is done when there is sentence "webpack xxx compiled successfully in xxx ms". After that, don't close the terminal. If it is accidentally closed, do make serve again

Next step:

  • Open new terminal
  • Do commands below
make up
make dev

Wait until it is on.

The web can be accessed using http://localhost:5000/

If the web is taking long time to load, restart cloud_native_gis_dev_1 container. The sequence should be make dev, after that run or restart cloud_native_gis_dev_1.

docs.title: Maputnik updates
docs.text:
CloudNativeGIS using maputnik to edit style. We could update maputnik in the folder root/maputnik. Maputnik can be accessed in the django-admin and layers, and there is "editor" column that will be redirect to maputnik instance. By default, it is using maputnik production.

To change and test maputnik:

make serve-maputnik

After it is done, there will be link to maputnik. Copy the link and paste in: go to deployment/.env change MAPUTNIK_URL to the copied link

restart dev container

After done, we need to update the maputnik production. First, create commit of maputnik and push it to repo. Then

make build-maputnik

It will create files in the django_project/cloud_native_gis/templates/maputnik.html and also assets in the django_project/cloud_native_gis/static

After that, test it by remove MAPUTNIK_URL and restart dev container. If satisfied, just create commit for the changes.

docs.title: Administrator Guide
docs.text: This guide covers the installation, configuration, and maintenance of Cloud Native GIS.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Cloud Native GIS is a Django-based platform that can be deployed as:

  • A standalone application using Docker
  • A Django library integrated into existing projects

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• Deployment - Installation and deployment options
  • Configuration - Environment variables and settings
  • Maintenance - Backup, monitoring, and troubleshooting

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text: Component Requirement CPU 2 cores RAM 4 GB Storage 20 GB SSD OS Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+, Debian 11+)

docs.title: Recommended Requirements
docs.text: Component Requirement CPU 4+ cores RAM 8+ GB Storage 100+ GB SSD OS Linux with Docker

docs.title: Software Dependencies
docs.text:
• Python: 3.10, 3.11, or 3.12
  • PostgreSQL: 13+ with PostGIS 3.0+
  • Docker: 20.10+ (for containerized deployment)
  • Node.js: 18+ (for frontend development)

docs.title: Docker Deployment
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/CloudNativeGIS
cd CloudNativeGIS

# Configure environment
cp deployment/.template.env deployment/.env
cp deployment/docker-compose.override.template deployment/docker-compose.override.yml

# Edit .env with your settings
nano deployment/.env

# Start the application
make up

docs.title: Django Library Installation
docs.text:
pip install cloud-native-gis

Add to your Django settings:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    'cloud_native_gis',
    # ...
]

Add to your URLs:

urlpatterns = [
    # ...
    path('', include('cloud_native_gis.urls')),
]

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
1. Change default credentials immediately after installation
  2. Use HTTPS in production
  3. Configure CORS appropriately for your domain
  4. Set secure environment variables (see Configuration)
  5. Regular security updates for all dependencies

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
• GitHub Issues
  • Kartoza Support

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Cloud Native GIS is configured through environment variables and Django settings.

docs.title: Core Settings
docs.text: Variable Description Default Required DEBUG Enable debug mode False No SECRET_KEY Django secret key - Yes ALLOWED_HOSTS Comma-separated allowed hosts localhost Yes (prod)

docs.title: Database Settings
docs.text: Variable Description Default DATABASE_URL Full database URL - DATABASE_NAME Database name django DATABASE_USERNAME Database user docker DATABASE_PASSWORD Database password docker DATABASE_HOST Database host db DATABASE_PORT Database port 5432

docs.title: Security Settings
docs.text: Variable Description Default CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS Trusted origins for CSRF - CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS Allowed CORS origins - CORS_ALLOW_ALL_ORIGINS Allow all CORS origins False

docs.title: Static Files
docs.text: Variable Description Default STATIC_URL Static files URL /static/ STATIC_ROOT Static files directory /home/web/static MEDIA_URL Media files URL /media/ MEDIA_ROOT Media files directory /home/web/media

docs.title: Maputnik Settings
docs.text: Variable Description Default MAPUTNIK_URL External Maputnik URL -

docs.title: Basic Configuration
docs.text:
# settings.py
from cloud_native_gis.settings import *

# Override settings as needed
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'False').lower() == 'true'
SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY')
ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_HOSTS', '').split(',')

docs.title: REST Framework
docs.text:
REST_FRAMEWORK = {
    'DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES': [
        'rest_framework.authentication.SessionAuthentication',
        'rest_framework.authentication.TokenAuthentication',
    ],
    'DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES': [
        'rest_framework.permissions.IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly',
    ],
    'DEFAULT_PAGINATION_CLASS': 'rest_framework.pagination.PageNumberPagination',
    'PAGE_SIZE': 100,
}

docs.title: CORS Configuration
docs.text:
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS = [
    "https://your-frontend.com",
    "http://localhost:3000",
]

# Or allow all (not recommended for production)
CORS_ALLOW_ALL_ORIGINS = True

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'disable_existing_loggers': False,
    'handlers': {
        'console': {
            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
        },
        'file': {
            'class': 'logging.FileHandler',
            'filename': '/var/log/cloudnativegis/app.log',
        },
    },
    'root': {
        'handlers': ['console', 'file'],
        'level': 'INFO',
    },
    'loggers': {
        'cloud_native_gis': {
            'handlers': ['console', 'file'],
            'level': 'DEBUG',
            'propagate': False,
        },
    },
}

docs.title: Configuration Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Never commit .env files to version control
  2. Use strong secrets for SECRET_KEY in production
  3. Limit ALLOWED_HOSTS to actual domains
  4. Configure CORS carefully for API security
  5. Enable logging for troubleshooting
  6. Use environment-specific settings (dev, staging, prod)

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Deployment Guide
docs.text: This guide covers various deployment options for Cloud Native GIS.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker 20.10+
  • Docker Compose 2.0+
  • Git

docs.title: Step-by-Step Installation
docs.text:
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/CloudNativeGIS
cd CloudNativeGIS

# 2. Initialize submodules
git submodule update --init --recursive

# 3. Copy configuration templates
cp deployment/.template.env deployment/.env
cp deployment/docker-compose.override.template deployment/docker-compose.override.yml

# 4. Edit environment variables
nano deployment/.env

# 5. Start the application
make up

docs.title: Docker Services
docs.text: Service Description Port nginx Reverse proxy 80 django Application server 5000 db PostgreSQL/PostGIS 5432 dev Development server 5000

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
1. Open http://localhost/ in your browser
  2. Access admin at http://localhost/admin/
  3. Default credentials are in your .env file

docs.title: Using Docker Compose
docs.text:
# docker-compose.prod.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  nginx:
    image: kartoza/cloud_native_gis:latest
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./ssl:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro
    depends_on:
      - django

  django:
    image: kartoza/cloud_native_gis:latest
    restart: always
    env_file:
      - .env.prod
    depends_on:
      - db

  db:
    image: kartoza/postgis:15-3.3
    restart: always
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    env_file:
      - .env.prod

volumes:
  pgdata:

docs.title: Environment Variables for Production
docs.text:
# .env.prod
DEBUG=False
SECRET_KEY=your-very-secure-secret-key
ALLOWED_HOSTS=your-domain.com,www.your-domain.com
DATABASE_URL=postgis://user:password@db:5432/cloudnativegis
CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS=https://your-domain.com

docs.title: SSL/TLS Configuration
docs.text:
For HTTPS, use Let's Encrypt with certbot:

# Install certbot
apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx

# Obtain certificate
certbot --nginx -d your-domain.com

docs.title: Helm Chart (Coming Soon)
docs.text: A Helm chart for Kubernetes deployment is planned for future releases.

docs.title: Basic Kubernetes Manifests
docs.text:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cloud-native-gis
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cloud-native-gis
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cloud-native-gis
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: kartoza/cloud_native_gis:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5000
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: cloud-native-gis-secrets

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install cloud-native-gis

docs.title: Django Settings
docs.text:
# settings.py
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    'django.contrib.gis',
    # Cloud Native GIS
    'cloud_native_gis',
    # Required dependencies
    'rest_framework',
    'rest_framework_gis',
    'corsheaders',
]

# Database with PostGIS
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.contrib.gis.db.backends.postgis',
        'NAME': 'your_database',
        'USER': 'your_user',
        'PASSWORD': 'your_password',
        'HOST': 'localhost',
        'PORT': '5432',
    }
}

docs.title: URL Configuration
docs.text:
# urls.py
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('gis/', include('cloud_native_gis.urls')),
]

docs.title: Run Migrations
docs.text: python manage.py migrate

docs.title: Horizontal Scaling
docs.text:
For high-traffic deployments:

  1. Use a load balancer (nginx, HAProxy, or cloud LB)
  2. Run multiple Django instances
  3. Use Redis for caching and Celery broker
  4. Consider a managed PostgreSQL service

docs.title: Performance Tuning
docs.text:
# settings.py
CACHES = {
    'default': {
        'BACKEND': 'django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache',
        'LOCATION': 'redis://redis:6379/1',
    }
}

# Tile caching
TILE_CACHE_TIMEOUT = 3600  # 1 hour

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text: This guide covers ongoing maintenance tasks for Cloud Native GIS.

docs.title: Using Docker
docs.text:
# Backup PostgreSQL database
docker compose exec db pg_dump -U postgres cloudnativegis > backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

# Compress backup
gzip backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

docs.title: Automated Backups
docs.text:
Create a cron job for regular backups:

# /etc/cron.d/cloudnativegis-backup
0 2 * * * root docker compose -f /path/to/docker-compose.yml exec -T db pg_dump -U postgres cloudnativegis | gzip > /backups/db_$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).sql.gz

docs.title: Database Restore
docs.text:
# Restore from backup
gunzip -c backup_20240101.sql.gz | docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres cloudnativegis

docs.title: Media Files Backup
docs.text:
# Backup media directory
tar -czvf media_backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz /path/to/media/

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
Cloud Native GIS provides health check endpoints:

# Check application health
curl http://localhost/health/

# Check database connectivity
curl http://localhost/health/db/

docs.title: View Container Logs
docs.text:
# All logs
docker compose logs -f

# Specific service
docker compose logs -f django

# Last 100 lines
docker compose logs --tail=100 django

docs.title: Log Locations
docs.text: Service Log Location Django Container stdout/stderr Nginx /var/log/nginx/ PostgreSQL Container stdout/stderr

docs.title: Database Queries
docs.text:
-- Check slow queries
SELECT pid, now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start AS duration, query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE (now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start) > interval '5 seconds';

docs.title: Tile Cache Statistics
docs.text: Monitor cache hit rates through your caching solution (Redis, etc.).

docs.title: Updating Docker Images
docs.text:
# Pull latest images
docker compose pull

# Restart with new images
docker compose down
docker compose up -d

# Run migrations
docker compose exec django python manage.py migrate

docs.title: Updating the Library
docs.text:
# Upgrade pip package
pip install --upgrade cloud-native-gis

# Run migrations
python manage.py migrate

# Collect static files
python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

docs.title: Container Won't Start
docs.text:
# Check container status
docker compose ps

# View logs
docker compose logs django

# Check for port conflicts
netstat -tlnp | grep :5000

docs.title: Database Connection Errors
docs.text:
# Test database connectivity
docker compose exec django python manage.py dbshell

# Check database status
docker compose exec db pg_isready -U postgres

docs.title: Migration Errors
docs.text:
# Show migration status
docker compose exec django python manage.py showmigrations

# Fake a migration if needed
docker compose exec django python manage.py migrate cloud_native_gis --fake 0001_initial

docs.title: Static Files Not Loading
docs.text:
# Collect static files
docker compose exec django python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Check nginx configuration
docker compose exec nginx nginx -t

docs.title: Debug Mode
docs.text:
Enable debug mode temporarily for troubleshooting:

# In .env
DEBUG=True

Warning

Never enable debug mode in production for extended periods.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
1. Check the GitHub Issues
  2. Review the Documentation
  3. Contact Kartoza Support

docs.title: Regular Updates
docs.text:
1. Update Docker images monthly
  2. Update Python dependencies quarterly
  3. Apply security patches immediately

docs.title: Security Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Change default passwords
  • [ ] Enable HTTPS
  • [ ] Configure firewall rules
  • [ ] Set up fail2ban for SSH
  • [ ] Enable database SSL
  • [ ] Review CORS settings
  • [ ] Audit user accounts

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: For Developers
docs.text: This is the homepage for all developer related documentation.

docs.title: Index
docs.text: title: Automatically Generated Python Documentation summary: DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE MANUALLY : It is created during the mkdocs build process date: 2023-08-03

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Cloud Native GIS User Guide. This section provides comprehensive documentation for end users working with the platform.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to Cloud Native GIS? Start here:

  • Getting Started - Your first steps with Cloud Native GIS
  • Working with Layers - Understanding and managing geospatial layers
  • Styling Your Maps - Using Maputnik to create beautiful map styles

docs.title: Layers
docs.text:
Cloud Native GIS supports both vector and raster layers, served as tiles for efficient map rendering.

  • Vector Layers: Point, line, and polygon data stored in PostGIS and served as vector tiles
  • Raster Layers: Image data served as Cloud Optimized GeoTIFFs (COG) or PMTiles

docs.title: Styles
docs.text:
Map styles are stored in the database using the Mapbox Style Specification. This allows for:

  • Consistent styling across all map views
  • Version control of style changes
  • Easy sharing of styles between layers

docs.title: Maputnik Integration
docs.text:
Maputnik is the built-in style editor that allows you to:

  • Visually design map styles
  • Preview changes in real-time
  • Export styles in standard Mapbox GL format

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
Topic Description Installation Quick installation guide API Reference REST API documentation Administration System administration guide

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide will help you get started with Cloud Native GIS, from your first login to creating your first map layer.

docs.title: Accessing the Platform
docs.text:
After installation, access Cloud Native GIS through your web browser:

  • Production: http://your-domain.com/
  • Development: http://localhost:5000/

docs.title: Django Admin Interface
docs.text:
Cloud Native GIS uses Django's admin interface for layer and style management. Access it at:

http://your-domain.com/admin/

docs.title: Logging In
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the admin URL
  2. Enter your username and password
  3. Click "Log in"

Note

Contact your administrator if you don't have credentials.

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare Your Data
docs.text:
Cloud Native GIS supports various geospatial formats:

  • Shapefiles (.shp with accompanying files)
  • GeoJSON (.geojson, .json)
  • GeoPackage (.gpkg)
  • KML (.kml)

docs.title: Step 2: Upload the Layer
docs.text:
1. Go to Cloud Native GIS > Layers in the admin
  2. Click Add Layer
  3. Fill in the layer details:
  4. Name: A descriptive name for your layer
  5. Description: Optional description
  6. File: Upload your geospatial file
  7. Click Save

docs.title: Step 3: Configure the Layer
docs.text:
After upload, the layer will be processed and you can:

  • View the layer attributes
  • Set the default style
  • Configure tile serving options

docs.title: Viewing Your Layer
docs.text:
Once a layer is created, you can:

  1. Preview in Admin: Click on the layer to see a map preview
  2. Access via API: Use the REST API to fetch layer data
  3. Embed in Applications: Use the tile URLs in your mapping application

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Working with Layers - Deep dive into layer management
  • Styling Your Maps - Learn to style your layers
  • API Reference - Integrate with your applications

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Working with Layers
docs.text: Layers are the fundamental building blocks of Cloud Native GIS. This guide covers everything you need to know about creating, managing, and serving geospatial layers.

docs.title: Vector Layers
docs.text:
Vector layers contain geometric features (points, lines, polygons) with associated attributes.

Supported formats:

  • Shapefile (.shp)
  • GeoJSON (.geojson)
  • GeoPackage (.gpkg)
  • KML (.kml)
  • CSV with geometry (.csv)

Storage: Vector data is stored in PostGIS and served as Mapbox Vector Tiles (MVT).

docs.title: Raster Layers
docs.text:
Raster layers contain gridded data such as satellite imagery, elevation models, or thematic maps.

Supported formats:

  • GeoTIFF (.tif, .tiff)
  • Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF (COG)
  • PMTiles (.pmtiles)

docs.title: Creating a Layer
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Upload File] --> B[Process Data]
    B --> C[Store in PostGIS]
    C --> D[Generate Tiles]
    D --> E[Apply Style]
  1. Navigate to Admin > Cloud Native GIS > Layers
  2. Click Add Layer
  3. Fill in the required fields:
  4. Name: Unique identifier for the layer
  5. Title: Display name
  6. Description: Layer description
  7. File: Your geospatial data file

docs.title: Layer Properties
docs.text: Property Description name Unique layer identifier title Human-readable display name description Layer description created_at Creation timestamp updated_at Last modification timestamp bbox Bounding box (auto-calculated) srid Spatial Reference ID

docs.title: Updating a Layer
docs.text:
To update layer data:

  1. Go to the layer in admin
  2. Upload a new file
  3. The old data will be replaced

Warning

Updating a layer replaces all existing features. Consider creating a new layer version instead.

docs.title: Deleting a Layer
docs.text:
1. Select the layer(s) in the admin list
  2. Choose "Delete selected layers" from the action dropdown
  3. Confirm deletion

Danger

Layer deletion is permanent and cannot be undone.

docs.title: Vector Tiles (MVT)
docs.text:
Vector layers are served as Mapbox Vector Tiles:

/api/v1/layer/{layer_id}/tile/{z}/{x}/{y}.mvt

docs.title: Raster Tiles
docs.text:
Raster layers support multiple formats:

# PNG tiles
/api/v1/layer/{layer_id}/tile/{z}/{x}/{y}.png

# JPEG tiles
/api/v1/layer/{layer_id}/tile/{z}/{x}/{y}.jpg

docs.title: PMTiles
docs.text:
For PMTiles layers, use the direct PMTile endpoint:

/api/v1/pmtile/{layer_id}/{path}

docs.title: Viewing Attributes
docs.text:
Each vector layer has associated attributes that can be:

  • Viewed in the admin interface
  • Queried via the API
  • Used for styling rules

docs.title: Attribute Types
docs.text: Type Description string Text values integer Whole numbers float Decimal numbers boolean True/False values date Date values datetime Date and time values

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use descriptive names: Choose clear, meaningful layer names
  2. Optimize data: Simplify geometries for web display
  3. Set appropriate zoom levels: Configure min/max zoom for performance
  4. Apply styles: Create styles to enhance visualization
  5. Document layers: Add descriptions and metadata

Made with by Kartoza | Donate! | GitHub

docs.title: Styling Your Maps
docs.text: Cloud Native GIS uses the Mapbox Style Specification for styling layers. The built-in Maputnik editor provides a visual interface for creating and editing styles.

docs.title: Understanding Map Styles
docs.text:
A map style defines how layers are rendered, including:

  • Colors: Fill, stroke, and text colors
  • Sizes: Line widths, symbol sizes
  • Typography: Font families, sizes, and placement
  • Filters: Data-driven styling rules
  • Zoom levels: Scale-dependent rendering

docs.title: Accessing Maputnik
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin > Cloud Native GIS > Layers
  2. Click on a layer
  3. Click the Editor link in the layer detail view

docs.title: Creating a Style
docs.text:
1. Select a layer from the sources panel
  2. Add a style layer for the data source
  3. Configure properties:
  4. Paint properties (visual appearance)
  5. Layout properties (positioning)
  6. Filter expressions (data selection)
  7. Preview your changes in real-time
  8. Save the style

docs.title: Fill Layers (Polygons)
docs.text:
{
  "fill-color": "#3388ff",
  "fill-opacity": 0.6,
  "fill-outline-color": "#000000"
}

docs.title: Line Layers
docs.text:
{
  "line-color": "#ff0000",
  "line-width": 2,
  "line-opacity": 1,
  "line-dasharray": [2, 4]
}

docs.title: Symbol Layers (Points/Labels)
docs.text:
{
  "icon-image": "marker",
  "icon-size": 1.5,
  "text-field": ["get", "name"],
  "text-size": 12,
  "text-color": "#333333"
}

docs.title: Data-Driven Styling
docs.text: Use expressions to style features based on their attributes:

docs.title: Color by Category
docs.text:
{
  "fill-color": [
    "match",
    ["get", "type"],
    "residential", "#ffcc00",
    "commercial", "#0066ff",
    "industrial", "#ff6600",
    "#cccccc"
  ]
}

docs.title: Size by Value
docs.text:
{
  "circle-radius": [
    "interpolate",
    ["linear"],
    ["get", "population"],
    0, 5,
    100000, 20,
    1000000, 40
  ]
}

docs.title: Zoom-Dependent Styling
docs.text:
{
  "line-width": [
    "interpolate",
    ["linear"],
    ["zoom"],
    10, 1,
    15, 4,
    20, 8
  ]
}

docs.title: Style Storage
docs.text:
Styles are stored in the database and linked to layers. Each layer can have:

  • One default style
  • Multiple named style variants

docs.title: Exporting Styles
docs.text:
1. Open the layer in Maputnik
  2. Click Export in the menu
  3. Download as JSON

docs.title: Importing Styles
docs.text:
1. Open the layer in Maputnik
  2. Click Import
  3. Upload your JSON style file

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start simple: Begin with basic colors, then add complexity
  2. Use zoom levels: Adjust detail based on zoom
  3. Consider accessibility: Use color-blind friendly palettes
  4. Test on different devices: Ensure styles work across screens
  5. Document your styles: Add comments and descriptions
